regulator: CBUAE (Central Bank of the UAE -- federal, own regulation of Payment Token Services applying UAE-wide but expressly excluding the Financial Free Zones, i.e. the DIFC and ADGM) / VARA (Virtual Assets Regulatory Authority -- Emirate of Dubai, including its free zones, excluding the DIFC) / DFSA (Dubai Financial Services Authority -- DIFC only) / FSRA (Financial Services Regulatory Authority -- ADGM only) / CMA (Capital Markets Authority -- federal, UAE-wide outside the DIFC and ADGM; legal successor to the Securities and Commodities Authority (SCA) since 1 January 2026)
instruments: CBUAE Payment Token Services Regulation (Circular No. C 2/2024) -- Effective from 31/8/2024, Status: In-Force -- lays down conditions for a License (Dirham Payment Token Issuer, Payment Token Custodian and Transferor, or Payment Token Conversion, per Article (5)) or a Foreign Payment Token Issuer Registration (Article (9), open to persons not incorporated and located in the UAE, expressly including persons in a Financial Free Zone); Article 2 prohibits, within 'the UAE' -- a term the Regulation itself defines, for the purposes of this Article, to exclude 'the jurisdiction of the Financial Free Zones' (i.e. DIFC and ADGM) -- both the performance of unlicensed Payment Token Services and the issuance of, or provision of services relating to, Algorithmic Stablecoins or Privacy Tokens, expressly stating this prohibition applies even to persons licensed or regulated by SCA or a Local Licensing Authority (e.g. VARA); Article (12) confines Dirham Payment Tokens to AED denomination and Foreign Payment Tokens to foreign-currency denomination and bars interest/time-based benefits to holders; Article (40) sets a one-calendar-year Transition Period running from the Regulation's commencement (i.e. to 31 August 2025), extendable at the Central Bank's discretion. All confirmed by direct fetch of the live CBUAE Rulebook, https://rulebook.centralbank.ae/en/rulebook/payment-token-services-regulation and its constituent Introduction, Article (2), Article (5), Article (9), Article (12) and Article (40) pages.

VARA Virtual Asset Issuance Rulebook, Annex 1: Fiat-Referenced Virtual Assets Issuance Rules (FRVA Rules) -- current version dated 19 May 2025 per the rulebook's own PDF filename (VARA_EN_293_VER20250519.pdf) -- applicable under VARA's rules, in the Emirate of Dubai (all zones including Special Development Zones and Free Zones, but excluding the DIFC, per VARA's own 'Emirate' definition in Schedule 2 of the VA Issuance Rulebook). Rule III.B.1 requires a VASP Licensed to issue an FRVA to hold Reserve Assets such that the FRVA is at all times at least 100% backed; Rule III.B.2 restricts Reserve Assets to cash/cash-equivalents or short-dated, highly-liquid instruments (debt securities of <=90 days residual maturity, repos of <=7 days, short-term government money-market funds); Rule III.C.2 requires redemption at par within one Working Day of a valid request, without fees; Rule III.D.1 requires a monthly independent audit of FRVA-in-circulation and Reserve Asset composition/value, attested to VARA by Senior Management; Rule III.F.1 sets minimum Paid-Up Capital at AED 1,500,000 plus 2% of the value of available FRVA supply; Rule I.C (Significant FRVA Issuer) lets VARA designate larger issuers for enhanced scrutiny based on factors including holder count, circulating value, and interconnectedness. Confirmed by direct fetch of https://rulebooks.vara.ae/rulebook/annex-1-fiat-referenced-virtual-assets-issuance-rules and its Part I (A. Interpretation / B. General Requirements / C. Significant FRVA Issuers), Part II (A. Additional Whitepaper Disclosures / B. Additional Ongoing Disclosures) and Part III (A. Maintenance of Stable Backing / B. Reserve Assets / C. Redemptions / D. Audits and Reporting / E. Marketing / F. Capital Requirements) sub-pages.

VARA VA Issuance Rulebook, Schedule 2 -- Definitions -- defines 'Fiat-Referenced Virtual Asset' or 'FRVA' as a Virtual Asset purporting to maintain a stable value relative to one or more fiat currencies (or other FRVAs), not having legal tender status in the UAE, and not issued for use as a means of payment for goods or services in the UAE; and states in terms, 'For the avoidance of doubt, and as stated in Rule I.B.4 of the FRVA Rules, the issuance of any FRVA that purports to maintain a stable value in relation to the value of AED shall not be approved under this VA Issuance Rulebook or the FRVA Rules, and shall remain under the sole and exclusive regulatory purview of the CBUAE' -- direct textual confirmation, from VARA's own rulebook, of the jurisdictional boundary between VARA's FRVA regime and the CBUAE's Payment Token Services Regulation for AED-referenced stablecoins. Confirmed by direct fetch, https://rulebooks.vara.ae/rulebook/schedule-1-definitions-0.

DFSA Rulebook, General Module (GEN), Chapter 3A 'Crypto Token Requirements' -- applicable under DFSA rules, only within the DIFC. GEN 3A.2.1 prohibits specified DIFC financial-services/promotion/fund activity in relation to a Crypto Token unless, for a non-fiat Crypto Token, the Person has itself undertaken and documented a reasoned suitability assessment, or, for a Fiat Crypto Token, 'the DFSA is satisfied that the Fiat Crypto Token is suitable for use in the DIFC' -- i.e. self-assessment for ordinary tokens but direct DFSA verification specifically for Fiat Crypto Tokens; GEN A2.5.5 defines a 'Fiat Crypto Token' as a Crypto Token whose value, to stabilise or reduce its price volatility, 'purports to be determined by reference to a single fiat currency'; GEN 3A.2.2 prohibits DIFC financial services, promotion, offers, or fund exposure relating to a 'Privacy Token' or 'Privacy Device' (each defined in GEN 3A.1.1); GEN 3A.2.3 separately prohibits the same range of activity in relation to an 'Algorithmic Token' (GEN 3A.1.1: a Crypto Token using or purporting to use an algorithm to alter Crypto Token supply to stabilise or reduce price volatility -- i.e. algorithmic stablecoins). All four provisions are stated, as fetched, to derive from DFSA RMI328/2022 (made 29 June 2022) as most recently amended by DFSA RMI423/2025 (made 15 December 2025), current consolidated version VER71/01-26. Confirmed by direct fetch of https://dfsaen.thomsonreuters.com/rulebook/gen-3a21, gen-3a21a, gen-3a22, gen-3a23, gen-3a11 and gen-a255.

FSRA Conduct of Business Rulebook (COBS), Chapter 17.2A 'Accepted Fiat-Referenced Tokens' and Chapter 19A 'Fiat-Referenced Tokens' -- applicable under FSRA rules, only within ADGM. COBS 17.2.1 / 17.2A.1 restrict an Authorised Person conducting a Regulated Activity involving a Fiat-Referenced Token ('FRT') to 'Accepted' FRTs approved by the FSRA; COBS 19A.2.1 requires a white paper filed with the Regulator at least 20 days before an FRT's issuance, covering issuer details, redemption/interest rights, Reserve Investment composition, and risk disclosures; COBS 19A.4.1 requires redemption of a presented FRT at par, in the referenced fiat currency, no later than T+2 after Customer Due Diligence; COBS 19A.5.2 requires payment received for FRT issuance to be held either as Client Money or invested in Reserve Investments in a segregated Reserve Account with a Third-Party Agent; COBS 19A.6.1 requires per-token segregation of Reserve Assets where an issuer has issued more than one FRT; COBS 19A.7.1-19A.7.2 require Reserve Asset market value to at all times equal or exceed outstanding redemption claims, valued daily; COBS 19A.10.1 requires an annual audit of Reserve Investment composition/valuation and internal controls; COBS 19A.11.1 gives FRT holders a pro-rata claim against Relevant Money/Reserve Investments. The FSRA's own 'STABLECOINS' guidance page states (para 159) that 'the FSRA has implemented a dedicated framework for the issuance of Fiat-Referenced Tokens ... in ADGM' and (para 161) that not every asset marketed as a 'stablecoin' meets the FRT definition (e.g. one referencing a basket of assets rather than a single fiat currency). Confirmed by direct fetch of https://en.adgm.thomsonreuters.com/rulebook/cobs-1721, cobs-172a1, cobs-19a21, cobs-19a41, cobs-19a51, cobs-19a52, cobs-19a61, cobs-19a71, cobs-19a72, cobs-19a101, cobs-19a111 and https://en.adgm.thomsonreuters.com/rulebook/stablecoins-1.

Federal Decree-Law No. 33 of 2025 Concerning the Regulation of the Capital Market, and Federal Decree-Law No. 32 of 2025 Concerning the Capital Market Authority -- both reported to have entered into force 1 January 2026, repealing Federal Law No. 4 of 2000 Concerning the Emirates Securities and Commodities Authority and Market and establishing the CMA as the federal successor to the SCA. Genuine PDF copies of both Decree-Laws were confirmed to exist at the CMA's own official domain (https://www.uaecma.gov.ae/assets/download/86f55732/federal-decree-law-no-33-of-2025-en-regulation-of.aspx and https://www.uaecma.gov.ae/assets/download/63e30eb2/federal-decree-law-no-32-of-2025-en-capital-market-authority.aspx), but this research could not machine-extract their compressed PDF text this session -- see open_items.

CMA Decision No. 4/R.M/2026 -- reported by multiple independent secondary legal-commentary sources (not independently verified against CMA primary text in this research pass -- see open_items) to have been issued in early-to-mid February 2026, replacing the prior SCA-era virtual-asset framework and, stablecoin-relevant, excluding Algorithmic Stablecoins from the CMA's 'Official Virtual Asset List' and requiring 'Fiat-Backed Crypto Token' status to rest on 1:1 backing by high-quality liquid reserves subject to monthly CMA audit; applicable under the CMA's federal framework, UAE-wide outside the DIFC and ADGM.
standing_summary: Consistent with this jurisdiction's structural pattern -- five parallel regulators, each the sole authority within its own geographic slice -- there is no single 'UAE stablecoin law'; rather, stablecoin-relevant rulemaking sits primarily with the CBUAE, whose Payment Token Services Regulation (Circular No. C 2/2024, in force from 31 August 2024) is the centre of gravity for this pillar, with VARA, the DFSA and the FSRA each running their own narrower, geographically-bounded fiat-referenced-token regimes inside their own territory, and the CMA's newly federal framework touching the edges of the topic through its 2026 prohibition list. Under the Central Bank of the UAE's regulation, applicable UAE-wide but expressly excluding the Financial Free Zones (the DIFC and ADGM, per the Regulation's own Article 2 carve-out and its definitions of 'Financial Free Zones' and 'Third Country'), no person may perform a Payment Token Service without being Licensed (for a Dirham Payment Token Issuer, a Payment Token Custodian and Transferor, or a Payment Token Conversion Provider, under Article (5)) or Registered as a Foreign Payment Token Issuer (Article (9), open to non-UAE-incorporated persons including those in a Financial Free Zone); the same Article 2 flatly bans, again everywhere except the Financial Free Zones and expressly overriding any SCA/Local-Licensing-Authority licence a person might separately hold, the issuance of or provision of services relating to Algorithmic Stablecoins or Privacy Tokens (both terms defined in Article (1)); Dirham Payment Tokens must be AED-denominated and may only be issued to UAE residents, Foreign Payment Tokens must be foreign-currency-denominated, and a one-calendar-year Transition Period from the Regulation's commencement (Article (40)) -- ending, on the Regulation's own terms, 31 August 2025 unless extended at the Central Bank's discretion -- governed the run-in period; all of this is confirmed by this research's direct, article-by-article fetch of the live CBUAE Rulebook. Under VARA's rules, applicable in the Emirate of Dubai (including its free zones, excluding the DIFC), a VASP wishing to issue a Fiat-Referenced Virtual Asset (FRVA) needs Category 1 issuance permission under the Virtual Asset Issuance Rulebook's Annex 1 (the FRVA Rules), and must, per this research's direct fetch of each operative sub-rule, maintain Reserve Assets at least 100% backing the FRVA at all times (Rule III.B.1), confined to cash/cash-equivalents or short-dated high-quality liquid instruments (Rule III.B.2); process at-par redemption within one Working Day, fee-free (Rule III.C); commission a monthly independent audit of circulation and reserve composition, attested to VARA (Rule III.D); and hold minimum Paid-Up Capital of AED 1,500,000 plus 2% of outstanding FRVA supply (Rule III.F). Critically, VARA's own Schedule 2 definitions state in terms that an FRVA referencing AED 'shall not be approved under this VA Issuance Rulebook or the FRVA Rules, and shall remain under the sole and exclusive regulatory purview of the CBUAE' -- a direct, cross-regulator boundary statement in VARA's own text that this research treats as authoritative for how VARA's and the CBUAE's stablecoin regimes divide labour. Under DFSA rules, applicable only within the DIFC, the General Module's Chapter 3A draws a distinction this pillar turns on: for a Crypto Token that is not a Fiat Crypto Token, an in-scope Person may rely on its own documented suitability assessment (GEN 3A.2.1(2)(a)), but for a Fiat Crypto Token -- defined at GEN A2.5.5 as one whose value purports to be determined by reference to a single fiat currency -- 'the DFSA is satisfied that the Fiat Crypto Token is suitable for use in the DIFC' is itself the operative condition, meaning the DFSA, not the firm, is the gatekeeper for DIFC stablecoin use; the same Chapter separately and absolutely prohibits DIFC activity relating to a 'Privacy Token' or 'Privacy Device' (GEN 3A.2.2) and to an 'Algorithmic Token' (GEN 3A.2.3, defined at GEN 3A.1.1 as a token using an algorithm to alter supply in order to stabilise price -- i.e. an algorithmic stablecoin), all as most recently amended by DFSA RMI423/2025 (made 15 December 2025, consolidated as VER71/01-26 of the GEN module), confirmed by this research's direct fetch of each numbered GEN 3A rule. Under FSRA rules, applicable only within ADGM, the Conduct of Business Rulebook's Chapter 19A (Fiat-Referenced Tokens) and Chapter 17.2A (Accepted Fiat-Referenced Tokens) confine ADGM Regulated Activity involving an FRT to FSRA-'Accepted' FRTs (COBS 17.2.1/17.2A.1), and require, as directly fetched by this research, a pre-issuance white paper filed at least 20 days ahead (COBS 19A.2.1), at-par redemption within T+2 (COBS 19A.4.1), segregated Reserve Accounts or Client Money treatment for issuance proceeds (COBS 19A.5.2), per-token Reserve Asset segregation across multiple FRTs (COBS 19A.6.1), daily-valued Reserve Assets that must at all times equal or exceed outstanding redemption claims (COBS 19A.7.1-.2), and an annual audit of Reserve Investment composition and controls (COBS 19A.10.1); the FSRA's own 'STABLECOINS' guidance page (paras 159-161) confirms the framework's existence and notes that not every asset marketed as a 'stablecoin' meets the FRT definition (e.g. a multi-asset-referenced token would not). Finally, under the CMA's federal framework, applicable UAE-wide outside the financial free zones (DIFC and ADGM), two genuine federal instruments -- Federal Decree-Law No. 32 of 2025 (Capital Market Authority) and No. 33 of 2025 (Regulation of the Capital Market), both reported in force from 1 January 2026 and confirmed by this research to exist as real PDFs on the CMA's own domain, though their text could not be machine-extracted this session -- established the CMA as the SCA's federal successor; CMA Decision No. 4/R.M/2026, reported (via multiple independent secondary legal sources, not independently primary-verified in this research pass) to have followed in February 2026, is described as excluding Algorithmic Stablecoins from the CMA's 'Official Virtual Asset List' and requiring 1:1 high-quality-liquid-reserve backing, subject to monthly CMA audit, for any token to qualify as a 'Fiat-Backed Crypto Token' -- structurally the same algorithmic-stablecoin exclusion and reserve-backing logic found, on a primary-source basis, in the CBUAE's, VARA's, the DFSA's and the FSRA's own regimes, but flagged here as resting on secondary corroboration only pending direct verification of the CMA's own decision text. This pillar deliberately does not duplicate the general VASP/exchange licensing regimes of any of the five regulators (tracked in the Exchanges (VATP) pillar) or the CBUAE's Digital Dirham CBDC programme and bank prudential treatment of virtual-asset exposure (tracked in Banking & Money).

open_items: CMA Decision No. 4/R.M/2026's substantive content for this pillar (its exclusion of Algorithmic Stablecoins from the 'Official Virtual Asset List', the 1:1-backing/monthly-audit conditions reported for 'Fiat-Backed Crypto Token' status, the eight-activity licensing structure, and the implementation timeline) is sourced in this card only from multiple independent secondary legal-commentary publications (Horizons & Co, Mielo Group, Clyde & Co, Dentons, King & Spalding, CMS, among others), not from a directly fetched CMA primary-source page or PDF -- repeated attempts this session to reach a readable copy of the Decision itself, including browsing uaecma.gov.ae's regulations/circulars listings, did not surface primary text. Secondary sources also disagree on the precise timeline (one reports the Decision 'effective' from 13 February 2026; another reports a phased pilot-July-2026 / large-businesses-January-2027 / smaller-businesses-July-2027 / government-October-2027 rollout) -- this should be re-verified directly against the CMA's own published decision text before any date or numeric threshold from it is treated as exact.

Federal Decree-Law No. 33 of 2025 (Regulation of the Capital Market) and No. 32 of 2025 (Capital Market Authority) were confirmed this session to exist as genuine PDF documents at the CMA's own official domain, and secondary sources consistently report both entered into force 1 January 2026, repealing Federal Law No. 4 of 2000 (the SCA's founding law) -- but this research could not machine-extract either PDF's text this session (compressed content streams, the same limitation this project has previously logged for Japan's FSA PDFs and Switzerland's FINMA Guidance 06/2024 PDF), so no specific article of either Decree-Law is quoted or cited as a direct-fetch fact in this card; only the fact of the documents' existence at the CMA's domain, and their titles as reported by secondary sources and as shown in web-search result snippets, are relied on.

Secondary sources (CoinDesk, Ledger Insights, BingX and others) report that, as of the DFSA's 12 January 2026 Crypto Token rule update, exactly three Fiat Crypto Tokens are DFSA-recognised for DIFC use -- Circle EURC, Circle USDC, and Ripple RLUSD -- but dfsa.ae itself returned HTTP 403 to every direct fetch attempt this session (Cloudflare-protected), so this research could not locate or fetch a DFSA-published register confirming that named list directly. Per this project's named-entity rule, no specific token issuer is named in the standing_summary on the strength of secondary sourcing alone; if this pillar is revisited, a working fetch of DFSA's own Recognised/Fiat Crypto Token list (or of dfsaen.thomsonreuters.com's equivalent register page, if one exists) should replace this open item.

VARA's FRVA Rules PDF (VARA_EN_293_VER20250519.pdf) carries a 19 May 2025 date in its own filename, and this research successfully fetched the rulebook's page structure and the text of every operative FRVA sub-rule directly (unlike the PDF-extraction failures logged elsewhere in this project) -- but the claim that 'all provisions took effect by 19 June 2025' is sourced from secondary commentary (Aston VIP), not from an explicit effective-date statement this research found on the VARA rulebook pages themselves. Should be re-confirmed directly against VARA's own version-history/'Versions' page for the FRVA Rules before being treated as exact.

The CBUAE Rulebook's live Payment Token Services Regulation text, fetched directly this session (including Article 2's prohibitions, which state they bind persons 'licensed or regulated by SCA or a Local Licensing Authority'), still refers throughout to 'SCA' -- the UAE Securities & Commodities Authority -- rather than 'CMA', even though the SCA was reconstituted as the CMA effective 1 January 2026 per Federal Decree-Law No. 32 of 2025. This most plausibly reflects the CBUAE Rulebook's own text not yet having been updated to reflect the rename, rather than any live legal ambiguity about which body now holds the referenced powers, but it is noted here as an unreconciled naming discrepancy between two primary sources (CBUAE's own live Rulebook vs. the federal Decree-Laws) as observed on the dates each was fetched this session.

This pillar tracks only the fiat-referenced/payment-token-specific rules of the CBUAE, VARA, DFSA and FSRA; VARA's, DFSA's and FSRA's general virtual-asset/crypto-token licensing regimes (including the DFSA's broader 12 January 2026 Crypto Token rule overhaul beyond its Fiat/Privacy/Algorithmic Token provisions, and the FSRA's Accepted Virtual Assets framework generally) are tracked in the Exchanges (VATP) pillar and not duplicated here; the CBUAE's Digital Dirham CBDC programme and any CBUAE prudential guidance on banks' virtual-asset exposure are tracked in the Banking & Money pillar, not this one.
